A. If there exists at least one student who is understanding the material, then if a student is attending tutorials, they are understanding the material, and every student is studying.
B. If there exists at least one student who is understanding the material, then if a student is attending tutorials, they are understanding the material, but not every student is studying.
C. If there exists at least one student who is understanding the material, then if a student is attending tutorials, they are understanding the material, and if a student is revising, then they are studying.
D. If there exists at least one student who is understanding the material, then if a student is attending tutorials, they are understanding the material, but if a student is revising, then they are not studying.
explanation: From premise 3, we know there exists at least one student who is understanding the material. From premise 5, if there exists at least one student who is understanding the material, then if a student is attending tutorials, they are understanding the material. From premise 1, every student is studying. From premise 2, if a student is attending tutorials, then they are understanding the material. From premise 4, if (a student attending tutorials implies they are understanding the material), then every student is studying. From premise 7, if a student is revising, then they are studying. Therefore, if there exists at least one student who is understanding the material, then if a student is attending tutorials, they are understanding the material, and every student is studying (from premises 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 7), making option A correct.
translation_issues: Premise 4 and 5: Similar to the previous batch, these translate meta-logical conditionals (statements about truth) rather than simple propositional facts.

premises-NL: Students are allowed to change majors if their cumulative GPA is 7.0 or higher.
Students must complete at least 2 semesters in their current major before changing.
Accumulated credits must be at least 50% of the total credits of the current program.
The new major must have a professional similarity of 60% or higher, as determined by the professional council.
If students have taken major-specific courses, these courses must be assessed for knowledge equivalence with the new major.
The major change must be approved by the academic council after reviewing grades, professional competence, and personal aspirations.
The academic council only meets for review in March and September each year.
Students must submit their major change application at least 30 days before the council meeting.
Late applications require waiting until the next meeting and paying a 10% tuition fee penalty.
The major change fee is 50% of the current semesters tuition, unless the student is eligible for priority policies.
Scholarship students must be reviewed separately by the finance department before changing majors.
If the scholarship is revoked after changing majors, students must repay 25% of the scholarship value received.
Students must complete missing courses in the new major within 1 year, with a maximum of 15 additional credits.
Failure to complete make-up courses within the specified time will result in academic suspension.
If the cumulative GPA falls below 6.0 after changing majors, students will receive academic warnings for 2 consecutive semesters.
Academic warnings are only lifted if the GPA is above 6.5 in the next semester and there are no violations of school regulations.
Students can only change majors a maximum of 1 time, except in special cases approved by the council.
Accumulated courses are only retained if they are 70% compatible with the new major, otherwise they must be retaken.
Students eligible for policies or with disabilities receive a 20% discount on the major change fee and priority review.
If the new major requires an entrance exam, students must achieve at least 75% of the standard score.
The academic council can reject major changes if they discover students have falsified their applications.
Mai has a cumulative GPA of 7.2 and has completed 3 semesters in Information Technology.
Mai wants to switch to Biomedical Engineering, which has a 65% similarity.
Mai has completed 45/90 credits in her current major and submitted her application on time in August.
Mai has a 50% tuition scholarship and has not changed majors before.
Biomedical Engineering requires an entrance exam with a standard score of 80%.
Mai scored 78% on the new majors entrance exam.
question: Can Mai successfully change majors from Information Technology to Biomedical Engineering, considering the entrance exam score, scholarship, and make-up course requirements, while assessing the risks of academic warnings or scholarship loss if she doesn't meet the requirements within 1 year after the transfer?
explanation: No, Mai cannot successfully change majors to Biomedical Engineering. Although she meets many requirements—GPA of 7.2 (Premise 1, 22), 3 semesters (Premise 2, 22), 50% credits (Premise 3, 24), 65% similarity (Premise 4, 23), timely application in August for September (Premise 8, 24), and council approval is feasible (Premise 6)—she fails the entrance exam requirement. Biomedical Engineering requires an 80% standard score (Premise 26), and Premise 20 mandates at least 75% of that (60%), but interpreting the major’s specific 80% as the threshold, Mai’s 78% falls short. Additionally, her scholarship requires separate review (Premise 12, 24), and post-transfer risks include academic warnings if GPA drops below 6.0 (Premise 14) or scholarship repayment if revoked (Premise 13) due to unfinished make-up courses within 1 year (Premise 13). The exam failure prevents the change outright.
translation_issues: Premises 1-27: The FOL translations (e.g., 'IfGraduateStudent(x) -> IsEnrolled(x)') do not match the NL content (e.g., 'Students are allowed to change majors...'). The logic provided is unrelated to the premise content (mostly about doctoral requirements not mentioned in the NL).

From premise 5, if (there exists a student in the PE course implies not attending the PE course), then (not attending implies not attending this semester). Therefore, if all students are enrolled in the PE course, then if a person has not attended the PE course, they are not attending the PE course this semester, and there exists a student who is enrolled in the PE course (from premises 1, 2, 3, and 5), making option A correct.
